title: Catechist's Guide — Human Sexuality
html:
Catechist's Guide

For preparing and leading the class. For catechist use only.

Lesson summary

Estimated duration: 55-65 minutes (with activities, 90 minutes)

Central theme: Human sexuality is God's good gift, a vocation to love inscribed in the body as male and female. It is ordered toward union and procreation, lived according to each person's state of life through the virtue of chastity. The Sixth and Ninth Commandments protect the dignity of sexuality against sins of lust, pornography, masturbation, fornication, adultery, and homosexual acts — while calling every person, including those with same-sex attraction, to be treated with respect, compassion, and sensitivity.

Objectives:

  • That the student understands sexuality as a gift from God, not a problem to manage.
  • That they grasp chastity as integration and self-mastery for everyone, in every state of life.
  • That they can articulate why pornography, masturbation, and fornication are grave offenses against the dignity of the person.
  • That they learn to distinguish between unconditional love for persons with same-sex attraction and fidelity to the Church's teaching on sexual acts.
  • That they see marriage as a total, faithful, fruitful, and exclusive gift — a sacrament reflecting Christ's love for the Church.

Key verse: 1 Corinthians 6:19-20 («Your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit»)

Catechism references: CCC 2331-2400 (Sixth Commandment: 2331-2400; Ninth Commandment: 2514-2533)

Suggested class structure

Section Duration What the catechist does
1. Welcome and opening prayer 4 min Greet warmly. Set a tone of trust and openness — this topic brings vulnerability. Begin with a simple prayer to the Holy Spirit. Remind students this is a safe space; no one will be put on the spot.
2. The big question 8 min Pose the question: «Why did God create us male and female? What is the purpose of our sexuality?» Invite brief responses — what does the world say? What has the Church said that surprised you? Introduce the Theology of the Body as a framework: the body is a language, not an accident.
3. Main content (Part 1): Gift and chastity 15 min Present: sexuality as gift and vocation (Gn 1:27-28, 2:18-25). The two dimensions: unitive and procreative. Chastity defined: not repression but integration of sexuality into the person. Chastity for everyone — married, single, celibate. Self-mastery as freedom, not suppression.
4. Main content (Part 2): Commandments and offenses 12 min The Sixth Commandment as Jesus deepens it (Mt 5:27-28) — purity of heart, not just external acts. Offenses against chastity: lust, masturbation, fornication, pornography, prostitution, rape, homosexual acts (CCC 2351-2359). Present with clarity AND charity. Never from a posture of condemnation. Emphasize: the Church distinguishes between the person (always loved) and the act (which may be disordered).
5. Same-sex attraction and pastoral care 6 min CCC 2357-2359: treat with respect, compassion, and sensitivity; avoid every sign of unjust discrimination; call to chastity. This is the most sensitive moment of the class. Do not skip it. Do not reduce it to slogans. Hold truth and love together. If you feel unprepared, be honest: «This is hard, and the Church asks us to hold two things at once. Let's talk about what that looks like.»
6. Marriage: total gift open to life 8 min The four marks: total, faithful, fruitful, exclusive. Marriage as sacrament — Christ and the Church (Eph 5:25-33). The wedding at Cana (Jn 2:1-12): Jesus' first miracle at a wedding says everything about how much he values marriage. «What God has joined together, let no man separate» (Mt 19:6).
7. Group reflection or activity 8 min Choose an activity from the workbook. For younger groups: the «custody of the eyes» challenge. For older groups: the media audit discussion. If the group is mature enough, open a brief Q&A — but keep it contained and redirect private struggles to confession or one-on-one conversation.
8. Closing and prayer 4 min Summarize the key idea. Read 1 Cor 6:19-20 aloud. Close with the workbook prayer. Remind students that mercy is always available in Confession, and that every saint was once a sinner who kept getting up.

Notes for the catechist

The tone for this topic: truth in love

This is arguably the most sensitive topic in the entire moral section. Half your students are drowning in a hypersexualized culture that tells them chastity is impossible. The other half have been wounded by sexual sin — their own or others' — and carry shame. Some have same-sex attraction and have heard only condemnation from Christians. Some have been victims of abuse. You are not just teaching doctrine; you are stepping onto holy ground. Before you prepare this class, pray. Ask the Lord for the same heart he had with the woman caught in adultery: «Neither do I condemn you. Go and sin no more» (Jn 8:11). He spoke truth AND gave mercy. You must do both. If you can only do one or the other, pause and pray more. This class will fail if it is only rules. It will also fail if it is only affirmation without truth. The only thing that works is the integration of both — which is, fittingly, exactly what chastity does.

You are not a therapist; know your limits

If a student discloses abuse, addiction, or profound confusion about their sexual identity, your role is not to fix them. Listen with compassion. Thank them for their trust. Connect them to a priest, a Catholic counselor, or an appropriate resource. Never promise confidentiality if there is a risk of harm. Know your diocese's reporting protocols for abuse disclosures. Have the phone number of a trusted Catholic therapist and priest-confessor ready.

About the Theology of the Body

You do not need to be a John Paul II scholar to teach this class, but a basic grasp of the Theology of the Body transforms the entire presentation. The core insight is simple: the body is a «sacrament» — a visible sign of an invisible reality. The masculine and feminine body speaks a language. What does it say? It says that we are made for gift. Man's body says he is made to give himself. Woman's body says she is made to receive the gift and give life in return. Together, they form a «communion of persons» that images the Trinity. This is why sexual acts outside this total self-gift (marriage) are lies told in the language of the body: the body says «I give everything,» but the person is holding back. The body says «I am open to life,» but contraception closes that door. If you communicate nothing else, communicate this: the Church's teaching on sex is not a list of «no's.» It is a profound «yes» to love that is real.

About pornography (do not minimize this)

Pornography is not a minor issue. It is a global industry that generates billions of dollars by exploiting human beings — many of them trafficked, coerced, or desperate. It rewires the brain's reward system, making real relationships harder and requiring increasingly extreme material to achieve the same effect. The average age of first exposure is 11. If you are teaching adolescents or young adults, assume that most — male and female — have been exposed and many struggle regularly. Frame this not as «you are dirty» but as «you have been lied to by an industry that wants to steal your capacity for real love.» The Catechism calls pornography a «grave offense» (CCC 2354) because it perverts the conjugal act — the intimate gift of spouses to each other — and does grave injury to the dignity of the participants, the actors, and the viewers. Recovery is possible. Resources like Covenant Eyes, Strive, and Exodus 90 exist. Confession is the ordinary medicine. A spiritual director or accountability partner is essential.

About masturbation

The Catechism (CCC 2352) teaches that masturbation is «an intrinsically and gravely disordered act.» It contradicts the purpose of the sexual faculty, which is ordered toward self-giving love within marriage and openness to life. Many students, especially in a culture that treats masturbation as healthy and inevitable, will find this teaching hard. Present it pastorally: the Church is not trying to make you feel guilty about your body. She is pointing out that masturbation turns sexuality inward — it trains the heart in self-gratification rather than self-gift. It is a training ground for using others rather than loving them. The immaturity of the person, force of acquired habit, conditions of anxiety, and other factors can diminish moral culpability (CCC 2352). But the act remains objectively disordered. The path forward is not shame but freedom: confession, grace, and the slow work of re-training the heart toward gift rather than consumption.

About same-sex attraction

This requires particular care. CCC 2357-2359 is the reference text and should be read carefully before teaching. Key points: (1) The psychological genesis of homosexuality remains largely unexplained, and the Church does not claim to know «why» someone experiences same-sex attraction. (2) Persons with this inclination «must be accepted with respect, compassion, and sensitivity. Every sign of unjust discrimination in their regard should be avoided.» (3) These persons are called to chastity and «to fulfill God's will in their lives.» (4) Homosexual acts are «intrinsically disordered» and «under no circumstances can they be approved» (CCC 2357). The pastoral challenge: you will almost certainly have students who experience same-sex attraction, students who have friends or family members who do, and students who have absorbed culture-war postures (either for or against). Your role is to model what the Catechism actually says: love without compromise. The person is sacred. The act is not. This distinction is foreign to modern culture, which collapses identity into sexual acts. The Church refuses that collapse. A person is infinitely more than their sexual attractions. And every person — without exception — is called to chastity according to their state of life.

About marriage: the total gift

Marriage is not a lifestyle choice. It is a vocation, a sacrament, and the original paradigm of human sexuality. Before sin, before law, before any commandment, there was the gift of man and woman to each other (Gn 2:24). The four marks of Christian marriage are drawn from the nature of love itself: (a) Total — the spouses give everything, not a part. They do not hold back their fertility, their future, their freedom. (b) Faithful — the gift is exclusive and permanent. «What God has joined together, let no man separate» (Mt 19:6). (c) Fruitful — love by its nature gives life. This is biological (children) but also spiritual (holiness, mission, hospitality). (d) Exclusive — only between the spouses. Adultery and polygamy violate the very structure of the gift. The sacramentality of marriage means that Christian spouses do not merely symbolize Christ's love for the Church; they participate in it. The husband's body makes present Christ's self-gift. The wife's body makes present the Church's receptive fruitfulness. This is not poetry; it is sacramental theology.

Age adaptation

🌱 Seed (Children ages 7-10): At this age, the primary message is: your body is good because God made it. Boys and girls are different on purpose — their bodies tell a story about love and family. Use the language of «God's plan for love» rather than explicit sexual content. Scripture anchor: creation of Adam and Eve (Gn 1-2). The wedding at Cana. Emphasize: God made families from a mom and a dad who love each other. When children ask where babies come from, answer simply: «God gives a baby to a mom and dad when they give themselves to each other in a special way only married people share.» Do not introduce the topics of pornography or masturbation at this age unless forced by a specific question; if asked, respond: «That's something your parents should talk with you about. Let's talk to them together.» The virtue to emphasize: modesty — keeping private things private, treating your body with respect.

🌿 Sprouts (Pre-adolescents ages 11-13): This is the critical window. They are entering puberty, discovering their changing bodies, and facing the first wave of peer pressure and online exposure. They need to hear: what is happening to your body is good and normal. Those feelings are real, and they are meant for something beautiful, not something to be afraid of. Introduce chastity as strength and freedom: being able to say «no» to what isn't right and «yes» to what is. Speak directly about pornography — name it — because statistically many have already been exposed. Frame it as a trap, not as a mark of being broken: «This industry wants your brain hooked before you are old enough to consent. If you have seen it, you are not bad. You were targeted. But now you know, and you can choose differently.» Introduce the concept of «saving yourself for marriage» not as fear-based abstinence but as preparing an incredible gift. Modesty becomes about protecting something precious, not hiding something shameful. Have resources available for private follow-up: «If you want to talk more about any of this, I am here after class.»

🪴 Root (Adolescents ages 14-17): At this age they can handle the full doctrine if presented the right way. They are making real decisions about relationships, boundaries, and identity. The Theology of the Body resonates especially with this age group when presented as big ideas: the body as language, love as self-gift, sexuality as vocation. They are idealistic enough to respond to a high vision. Be explicit about the challenges: yes, chastity is hard. Yes, your culture thinks it is stupid. Yes, you will fail sometimes. And yes, the saints fell and got back up. Key points for this age: (a) The media audit activity (tracking what you consume and what it does to you). (b) The question of identity — you are not your desires, you are a child of God with desires that need integration. (c) Practical strategies: avoiding near occasions of sin, accountability, Confession as normal medicine (not the emergency room). (d) Vocation: marriage or consecrated life is not a distant future; you are becoming that person now. For groups with mixed-gender adolescents, consider splitting by gender for part of the discussion — the questions girls have and the questions boys have are often different, and same-gender space can increase honesty.

🌳 Tree (Adults 18+): Adults need the full theological and pastoral framework. They carry the wounds of past sexual sins, failed relationships, divorces, addictions, and cultural narratives they have absorbed for decades. Many have been away from the Church and are returning with baggage. The key with adults is to present the doctrine as liberation from lies they have believed, not as a new burden. Begin by acknowledging the damage the sexual revolution has done: loneliness, broken families, pornography addiction at epidemic levels, the commodification of bodies, the epidemic of fatherlessness. The Church's teaching is the antidote, not the disease. Topics to develop fully: (a) Humanae Vitae and the inseparable connection between the unitive and procreative meanings. (b) The nature of sacramental marriage versus civil marriage and cohabitation. (c) Same-sex attraction: the pastoral distinction between orientation (not chosen, not sinful in itself) and acts (objectively disordered). (d) The path of healing through the Sacraments, especially Confession and Eucharist. (e) Vocational discernment for the single, the widowed, and those who experience same-sex attraction. Use resources like the full Theology of the Body audiences, Christopher West's presentations, and Jason Evert's chastity talks. If the group includes married couples, invite them to share (without violating intimacy) what has helped them live chastity in marriage.

Supplementary resources

  • John Paul II, Theology of the Body (129 audiences, 1979-1984): The foundational text. Available in multiple translations. Christopher West's Theology of the Body for Beginners is the most accessible introduction; his Theology of the Body Explained is more comprehensive. For the visual learner, Ascension Press has excellent video series.
  • The Chastity Project (Jason and Crystalina Evert): Evert's talks («Love or Lust,» «Gender and the Theology of Your Body») are available on YouTube and are among the most effective presentations for adolescents and young adults. He combines humor, statistics, and a compelling vision of love.
  • Covenant Eyes: Accountability software for pornography recovery. Also produces excellent educational content on the neuroscience of porn addiction.
  • Strive — Exodus 90: Freedom-focused programs for men struggling with sexual sin. Exodus 90 (90 days of ascetic discipline) has helped thousands. For women: Well-Read Mom, Endow groups.
  • Eden Invitation: Catholic ministry accompanying persons who experience same-sex attraction, living chastity in community. Faithful, pastoral, non-political.
  • Courage International: The Church's official apostolate for persons with same-sex attraction who seek to live chastely. Also: EnCourage for their families and friends.
  • YouCat 400-427: Youth catechism treatment of the Sixth and Ninth Commandments. Direct, frank, age-appropriate language.
  • Love and Responsibility by Karol Wojtyla (John Paul II): Written before he became Pope, this is the philosophical foundation of the Theology of the Body. Dense but rewarding for adult groups.
  • Humanae Vitae (Paul VI, 1968): The encyclical on the regulation of birth. The prophetic text that predicted the consequences of separating sex from procreation. Worth reading in full.
  • Sisterhood of the Traveling Scapular (podcast): Sisters discussing real-life virtue with humor and depth.
  • Movies for discussion: Fireproof (2008, about fighting for a marriage), October Baby (2011, themes of dignity and healing), and the documentary The Heart of the Matter (on pornography).

Possible difficult questions (and how to answer them)

«Isn't the Church just obsessed with sex? Why so many rules about what I do with my body?»

The Church is not obsessed with sex. Modern culture is. The Church has a clear and consistent teaching about sexuality precisely because sex is powerful — it involves the whole person, body and soul, and has the capacity to create new immortal beings. Things that powerful need to be treated with reverence. If you left a nuclear reactor unguarded, you would not be «obsessed with safety» for putting up fences. The Church's teaching is a fence around something sacred, not a cage around something shameful. And look at what happens when sex is treated casually: broken hearts, broken families, addictions, exploitation, loneliness. The Church's rules are not arbitrary; they are guardrails on the road to love that lasts.

«If God made me with sexual desires, why is it wrong to act on them?»

God made you with hunger too. That does not mean eating anything at any time is good for you. Desires are real and they are good — they point toward something. Hunger points toward food; sexual desire points toward total self-gift in marriage open to life. The desire is not the problem; it's the direction. A disordered desire is like a compass that points slightly off true north. It is still a compass. It still wants to find north. But it needs to be calibrated. Chastity is the calibration. What the world calls «acting on desires» the Church calls «integration» — bringing those desires into the service of love rather than letting them drive you into using people.

«Why does the Church hate gay people?»

It doesn't. CCC 2358 says, word for word, that persons with same-sex attraction «must be accepted with respect, compassion, and sensitivity. Every sign of unjust discrimination in their regard should be avoided.» If any Catholic has treated you or someone you love with hatred or contempt, they were wrong, and they were acting against the Church's explicit teaching. What the Church cannot do is say that homosexual acts are good or morally equivalent to marital acts. That would be like a doctor who really loves his patient but refuses to tell her the tumor is malignant. The refusal to affirm what is disordered is not hatred — it is the uncomfortable form love sometimes takes. The Church calls all people to chastity. Married heterosexual couples are called to be faithful and open to life. Single heterosexuals are called to continence. Persons with same-sex attraction are called to chastity. The calling is different in shape, but it is the same in substance: ordering sexuality toward self-gift rather than self-gratification.

«How can you tell a person with same-sex attraction they have to be alone their whole life? Isn't that cruel?»

The Church does not say they must be alone. It says they must be chaste — and so must everyone who is not married. A single heterosexual person who is not called to marriage or religious life is also called to a life of chastity, and also may never marry. Loneliness is not caused by chastity; it is caused by the absence of deep friendship, community, and communion with God. Many chaste celibate people live lives rich with friendship, purpose, and love. The real cruelty would be telling someone that the only kind of love that counts is erotic love — that if you cannot have that, you cannot have love at all. The Church says the opposite: love is bigger than eros, and your capacity to love and be loved is not limited by your sexual attractions. Moreover, chaste friendship is one of the great treasures of Christian life, and it is available to everyone. The witness of groups like Courage and Eden Invitation shows that joyful, fulfilled, chaste lives are possible with same-sex attraction.

«The world has changed since the Bible was written. Doesn't the Church need to update its sexual morality?»

The Church has updated many things: the form of the liturgy, canon law, pastoral approaches, disciplinary practices. What it cannot update is moral truth that is grounded in the nature of the human person. The body has not changed since Genesis. The meaning of the sexual act — total self-gift open to life — has not changed. Technology has changed (contraception, IVF, pornography delivery systems). Cultural attitudes have changed. But the reality that sex makes babies and bonds spouses has not changed. The Church reads human nature the way a musician reads sheet music: you can play it in different styles, but you cannot change the notes and pretend it's the same piece. The world says the notes don't matter — only how you feel about the music. The Church insists the composer had something in mind, and playing it faithfully matters.

«I've already messed up. I'm not a virgin. I've looked at porn. Is it too late for me to live this?»

Absolutely not. This is the most important answer in the whole class. The Gospel is a story of people who failed and were restored. David committed adultery and murder, repented, and became the ancestor of the Messiah. The woman at the well had five husbands and a live-in boyfriend, and Jesus made her the first evangelist to Samaria. Peter denied Christ three times and became the first Pope. Sexual sin does not disqualify you from holiness. It means you take it to Confession, you receive absolution, and you start again. Chastity is not a prize you win once. It is a daily choice you make, and some days you will fail. The measure of a Christian is not that they never fall. It is that they keep getting back up. «A saint is just a sinner who keeps trying» (St. Josemaría Escrivá). If you have a habit of grave sin, seek help: a regular confessor, an accountability partner, perhaps professional counseling. But do not let shame convince you it is too late. It is never too late for mercy.

«What if I don't feel called to marriage or religious life? What is my vocation regarding sexuality?»

The single life lived in the world is a real and holy vocation. You are not in a «waiting room» until something better comes along. The Church teaches that single persons are called to chastity in continence, and that this state can be a profound witness to the Kingdom — it says that the ultimate fulfillment of every human heart is God, not a human spouse. Your chastity as a single person is not a deprivation; it is a gift of yourself to God and to the community in a particular way. Cultivate deep friendships. Serve in your parish. Pour yourself into your work and your family. Your sexuality is not on hold; it is being lived in a mode of generous availability that married people, by the nature of their vocation, cannot offer. Do not despise this gift. St. Paul considered it the higher calling (1 Cor 7:32-35).

Supplementary notes on doctrinal content

The Sixth and Ninth Commandments together

These two commandments form a pair: the Sixth («You shall not commit adultery») governs external acts; the Ninth («You shall not covet your neighbor's wife») governs the interior movements of the heart. Together they cover the full range of sexual morality — what you do and what you desire. Jesus binds them together in the Sermon on the Mount: the look of lust is already adultery of the heart (Mt 5:28). This means that purity is not merely about avoiding certain acts; it is about the conversion of desire itself. The Catechism treats the Ninth Commandment in CCC 2514-2533. The «battle for purity» (CCC 2520) is fought primarily in the heart and mind, through modesty, custody of the senses, and the purification of the «climate» of our imagination. For a complete treatment, you may wish to reference the Ninth Commandment as well.

The inseparable connection: unitive and procreative

This is the doctrinal heart of Catholic sexual ethics. The sexual act has two meanings, given by God, that cannot be separated without doing violence to the act itself: the unitive meaning (expressing and deepening the spouses' love) and the procreative meaning (openness to new life). Every act of marriage must remain open to both. This is why contraception is morally wrong: it deliberately severs the procreative from the unitive. Likewise, this is why IVF is morally wrong: it severs the unitive from the procreative («making» a baby in a lab rather than «begetting» a child through the act of love). Humanae Vitae (1968) defended this teaching prophetically, predicting that widespread contraception would lead to marital infidelity, lowered respect for women, and government coercion in reproductive matters — all of which have come to pass. Natural Family Planning (NFP), by contrast, works with the body's natural rhythms and respects the integrity of the act.

Self-mastery as freedom

A central theme throughout CCC 2337-2347 is that chastity requires self-mastery, and self-mastery is not repression but freedom. «The alternative is clear: either man governs his passions and finds peace, or he lets himself be dominated by them and becomes unhappy» (CCC 2339). This echoes the classical and biblical tradition: the person who cannot say «no» to himself is a slave, no matter how many external freedoms he has. The person who can govern his desires is free even in chains. This is why the Church can speak of chastity as a «joyful affirmation» (CCC 2346) — because the freedom to love authentically is more joyful than the slavery of unregulated passion.

Chaste celibacy as witness to the Kingdom

The celibate or consecrated person does not live chastity because sexuality is bad. They live it because the Kingdom of God is so real and so near that it relativizes even the great goods of marriage and family. The celibate says, with their body: «The marriage of Christ and the Church is the only marriage that ultimately satisfies. Everything else points to it.» This is why celibacy for the Kingdom is a gift, not a sacrifice grudgingly made. It is the anticipation of the life of heaven, where «they neither marry nor are given in marriage» (Mt 22:30). Present consecrated virginity and priestly celibacy not as a «higher» path in the sense of elitism, but as an eschatological sign — a billboard for the world to come.

Blessed Chiara Badano: a chaste witness for the young

Chiara «Luce» Badano (1971-1990) was an Italian teenager in the Focolare movement who died of bone cancer at age 18. She is particularly relevant to this lesson. As a teenager, Chiara was beautiful, athletic, and popular, and she chose to live chastity not as grim repression but as joy. When her boyfriend gave her a romantic gift, she said: «It's beautiful, but I don't feel it's right. Let's give it to the poor children.» She didn't break up with him; she redirected their love toward something purer. When cancer took her hair and her legs stopped working, she refused morphine because «it takes away my lucidity, and I have nothing left but Jesus to offer. I want to share his pain on the Cross.» Her last words to her mother: «Mama, good-bye. Be happy, because I am.» She was beatified in 2010. For adolescents who think chastity means missing out on life, Chiara is the perfect counter-witness. She never missed out on anything real.

To prepare: Read CCC 2331-2400 carefully. Have your Bible open to Gn 1-2, Mt 5:27-30, 1 Cor 6:18-20, Eph 5:25-33, and Jn 2:1-12. Pray for your students before you walk into the room. If possible, visit the Blessed Sacrament the day you teach this lesson. This topic needs grace, not just information.

Catechist's prayer

Lord Jesus, Bridegroom of the Church,
you who blessed marriage at Cana
and called the chaste heart to see God,
give me the wisdom to speak truth in love.
Where my students carry shame, let me be a channel of your mercy.
Where they carry confusion, let me be a voice of clarity.
Where they have been wounded by others' sins
or by their own, let me not add to their wounds.
Teach me to love as you love the woman caught in adultery:
neither condemning nor compromising.
Blessed Chiara Badano, intercede for these young hearts,
that they may believe, as you did,
that purity is worth everything
and that Jesus alone is enough.
Amen.

Print Catechist's Guide
